ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a hierarchical BiLSTM with residual (HR-BiLSTM) to match multi-level question representations to relation representations and integrates this into a two-step KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking. While the hierarchical matching plus shortcut connections and the application to entity re-ranking is a coherent and useful engineering contribution distinct from STAGG, AMPCNN and simple attention baselines, the techniques are largely incremental extensions of existing sequence-matching and residual-learning ideas rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Given the use of standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) and common baselines, the work would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts as a major conceptual advance. | B(3): The paper presents a targeted architectural improvement (HR-BiLSTM with residual shortcut learning and multi-level matching to relation word- and relation-level representations) applied to relation detection and integrated into a simple two-step KBQA pipeline. This is a meaningful engineering advance over prior relation classifiers (e.g., STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention baselines) but not a fundamentally new problem formulation or paradigm; the ideas (hierarchical representations, residual connections, re-ranking via relation scores) are sensible extensions rather than radical departures. From the KBQA research perspective, the work is an incremental but well-differentiated contribution that would be of practical interest to experts seeking improved relation scoring and entity re-ranking techniques.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (relation detection for KBQA) and targets the audience working on semantic parsing and QA at top venues: it proposes a new hierarchical residual BiLSTM architecture and integrates it into a two-step KBQA pipeline evaluated on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) against strong baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention models). The methodology, datasets, baselines, and metrics (accuracy/top-1) are those expected at a top conference, and the claimed improvements in end-to-end KBQA relevance make it highly topical and of interest to the community. From a venue-fit perspective the work is well positioned and appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it tackles relation detection for KBQA, proposes a novel hierarchical-residual matching model (HR-BiLSTM), and demonstrates empirical gains on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) against strong baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN). The methodology and evaluation (accuracy/top-1, FB2M subset) follow community standards, and the two-step pipeline that integrates entity re-ranking addresses practical end-to-end KBQA concerns that the venue’s audience cares about. Given the clear positioning, thorough baselines, and relevance to both single- and multi-relation KBQA, this is an appropriate and timely contribution for a top conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments compare against appropriate baselines on both SimpleQuestions and WebQSP and report hyperparameters and standard accuracy metrics, supporting the main claims to a degree. However the summary does not indicate thorough ablation studies isolating the contributions of hierarchical matching versus residual shortcuts or analyses of variance across runs, weakening causal attribution. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and a limitation (hierarchical attention for long sequences) is acknowledged, but code availability is not mentioned which limits reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Overall the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing important sensitivity/ablation analyses and full reproducibility artifacts. | B(4): The experiments cover both single- and multi-relation KBQA (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) and compare to relevant baselines, supporting the main claims about HR-BiLSTM and the two-step pipeline; hyperparameters are reported and a realistic FB2M KB is used. However the summary does not indicate explicit ablation studies isolating the contribution of hierarchical matching vs residual connections, and code is not reported which limits full reproducibility. Related work is described as thorough, and at least one limitation (attention for long sequences) is discussed, so overall the evaluation is solid with some minor gaps in detailed analysis and release of artifacts.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets an important, practical problem (relation detection for KBQA) and demonstrates empirical gains by combining hierarchical matching with residual learning (HR-BiLSTM) within a simple two-step pipeline, achieving state-of-the-art or comparable results on both SimpleQuestions and WebQSP. From my methodological perspective, the approach appears practically useful (entity re-ranking via relation scores and improved core-chain detection) and the authors report hyperparameters and compare to appropriate baselines, suggesting the results could change implementation choices in the area. While the contribution seems more evolutionary than transformative, it is a strong, well-scoped advancement likely to be adopted and extended by researchers and practitioners working on KBQA and relation detection. | B(3): This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck in KBQA — relation detection — and demonstrates measurable end-to-end gains (SOTA or comparable) on both single- and multi-relation benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using HR-BiLSTM and a simple two-step pipeline with entity re-ranking. From my KBQA landscape perspective, the contribution is a valuable, practically useful improvement that clarifies how hierarchical question representations and residual learning can aid relation/chain scoring and entity re-ranking. However, the advance appears incremental rather than transformative: it refines relation matching and integration into pipelines rather than changing how researchers fundamentally approach compositional or large-scale KB reasoning. The work is likely to be adopted by practitioners working on KBQA pipelines but does not by itself open broad new directions beyond the subfield.

While the contribution seems more evolutionary than transformative, it is a strong, well-scoped advancement likely to be adopted and extended by researchers and practitioners working on KBQA and relation detection.

The work is likely to be adopted by practitioners working on KBQA pipelines but does not by itself open broad new directions beyond the subfield.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical progression from problem motivation to method (HR-BiLSTM) and experiments (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) through to conclusions, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions (hierarchical matching, residual connections, two-step KBQA pipeline) are clear and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility, though the absence of mentioned code slightly reduces practical clarity. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and informative, and the writing quality is generally good with only minor points (e.g., a brief note that hierarchical matching with attention is left to future work) that could be clarified. Overall the presentation is strong with minor presentation issues that prevent an exceptional rating. | B(4): From the provided summary the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early — the HR-BiLSTM model and the two-step KBQA pipeline are easy to identify. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent, related work coverage is thorough, and experiments (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported clearly. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though the discussion of future work (e.g., attention for long sequences) hints at minor areas where more clarity or additional illustrative examples could improve presentation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and reasonably recent baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, an attention baseline) on the standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same accuracy metrics, and reports hyperparameters—supporting fair experimental conditions. The authors also candidly discuss limitations (e.g., hierarchical matching vs. attention for long sequences) and claim comparable or state-of-the-art performance. What is missing or unclear from the summary are stronger ablation studies attributing gains specifically to hierarchical matching versus residual shortcuts, and whether all relevant recent KBQA/relation-detection models were included; releasing code would further strengthen reproducibility. Overall comparisons are solid but could be improved with more targeted analyses and potentially a few additional baselines. | B(4): The paper compares to a solid set of established KBQA/relation-detection baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention baseline) on standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using common metrics, and reports hyperparameters — indicating generally fair comparisons. From a KBQA perspective, however, it appears to omit some potentially relevant recent or more specialized multi-relation/compositional baselines and does not mention code release for reproducibility; the authors do acknowledge when prior methods are comparable. Overall the empirical comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive for all contemporary competing approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and reasonably recent baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, an attention baseline) on the standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same accuracy metrics, and reports hyperparameters—supporting fair experimental conditions. The authors also candidly discuss limitations (e.g., hierarchical matching vs. attention for long sequences) and claim comparable or state-of-the-art performance. What is missing or unclear from the summary are stronger ablation studies attributing gains specifically to hierarchical matching versus residual shortcuts, and whether all relevant recent KBQA/relation-detection models were included; releasing code would further strengthen reproducibility. Overall comparisons are solid but could be improved with more targeted analyses and potentially a few additional baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to a solid set of established KBQA/relation-detection baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention baseline) on standard datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using common metrics, and reports hyperparameters — indicating generally fair comparisons. From a KBQA perspective, however, it appears to omit some potentially relevant recent or more specialized multi-relation/compositional baselines and does not mention code release for reproducibility; the authors do acknowledge when prior methods are comparable. Overall the empirical comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive for all contemporary competing approaches.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental results on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP showing improved relation detection and end-to-end KBQA performance, which matches the described methodology (HR-BiLSTM with residuals and a two-step pipeline). Mathematical content appears limited and appropriate for the model description; no apparent derivation issues in the summary. Experimental methodology is generally sound with reasonable baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention), but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance, multiple runs/variance reporting, code release, and stronger ablation studies to isolate the hierarchical and residual contributions—these are minor gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions. Overall the conclusions are plausible given the results, but would be strengthened by reproducibility assets and more granular experimental controls. | B(4): The paper’s central claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical results on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP using appropriate baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention), and the HR-BiLSTM design and two-step KBQA pipeline are technically plausible. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the experimental methodology is generally sound, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or released code which would strengthen reproducibility. Minor gaps include limited analysis of scalability/robustness (e.g., noisy entity linking) and the unexplored attention variants acknowledged by the authors, so I mark a minor deficiency rather than a fatal flaw.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental results on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP showing improved relation detection and end-to-end KBQA performance, which matches the described methodology (HR-BiLSTM with residuals and a two-step pipeline). Mathematical content appears limited and appropriate for the model description; no apparent derivation issues in the summary. Experimental methodology is generally sound with reasonable baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention), but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance, multiple runs/variance reporting, code release, and stronger ablation studies to isolate the hierarchical and residual contributions—these are minor gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions. Overall the conclusions are plausible given the results, but would be strengthened by reproducibility assets and more granular experimental controls.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical results on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP using appropriate baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention), and the HR-BiLSTM design and two-step KBQA pipeline are technically plausible. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the experimental methodology is generally sound, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or released code which would strengthen reproducibility. Minor gaps include limited analysis of scalability/robustness (e.g., noisy entity linking) and the unexplored attention variants acknowledged by the authors, so I mark a minor deficiency rather than a fatal flaw.

main_claims: An improved relation detection model using hierarchical matching between questions and relations with residual learning (HR-BiLSTM).

A simple two-step KBQA system that integrates entity re-ranking and the improved relation detector yields state-of-the-art or comparable results on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) tasks.
The HR-BiLSTM model enables entity re-ranking via relation scores and more accurate core-chain (relation chain) detection, improving end-to-end KBQA performance.
_summary.methodology: They propose a hierarchical recurrent neural network (HR-BiLSTM) with residual (shortcut) connections to learn multi-level question representations and match them to both relation-level and word-level relation representations; this model is used in a two-step KBQA pipeline with entity re-ranking and per-entity relation (or core-chain) scoring.
limitations: Authors note hierarchical matching with attention may work better for long sequences and leave this for future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Knowledge Base Question Answering / Relation Detection

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Evaluator
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating neural NLP systems, focusing on reproducible experimentation and robust empirical claims.
traits: Insists on clear experimental setup: datasets, splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, and training details to enable replication
Requires strong baselines and ablation studies to attribute gains to specific design choices (e.g., hierarchical matching vs residual connections vs model capacity)
Checks for appropriate evaluation metrics, statistical significance or variance reporting across runs, and careful error analysis
Values practical implementation details: inference cost, model size, latency, and how components (entity re-ranking, relation detector) interact in the pipeline
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Detail-oriented and constructive: verifies experimental rigor and reproducibility, points to missing controls or benchmarks, and suggests concrete experiments to strengthen empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: KBQA Landscape Analyst
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher specializing in knowledge-base question answering and semantic parsing, tracking methodological trends and practical impact across KBQA benchmarks and real-world systems.
traits: Evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior KBQA and relation-detection work, noticing incremental vs substantive advances
Values clear demonstration of where and why the proposed approach helps (e.g., single vs multi-relation, ambiguity resolution, rare relations, compositional queries)
Looks for broader applicability and limitations: scalability to large KBs, robustness to noisy entities, and integration with entity linking and reasoning components
Pays attention to significance for the community: whether the approach changes best practices, offers reusable components, or opens new research directions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-aware and impact-focused: situates contributions within the KBQA literature, assesses practical significance, and advises on framing and experiments to clarify novelty and limitations.
